a: Capital automation of physical sorting is relevant context but is not counted as AI labor substitution unless it avoids current labor within the five-year implementation path.
a: The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.

rho: Implementation feasibility differs sharply between high-throughput processors and small mixed-material yards.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat purchases of scrap and sales of graded secondary commodities to mills, foundries, processors, exporters, and manufacturers; economics combine commodity spread, processing and upgrading yield, logistics, scale aggregation, and sometimes recurring container, pickup, destruction, or compliance services.
lens.deviation_from_default: The highly heterogeneous code is narrowed to repeat-service merchants with recurring generator and consuming-mill relationships and accountable material-handling operations. Pure commodity brokers and used-parts-led dismantlers are excluded because they do not share a coherent recurring outsourced-service model.
lens.excluded_activities: Municipal collection-only operations, waste haulers and material-recovery facilities classified outside 423930, pure brokers that do not take title, captive industrial scrap units, shells, hazardous-waste specialists, and auto wreckers whose economics are principally retail used-parts sales rather than wholesale scrap flows.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market recyclable-material merchants with repeat industrial, commercial, municipal, or dealer counterparties that source, grade, process, aggregate, broker while taking title to, and sell ferrous metal, nonferrous metal, paper, plastics, electronics, automotive scrap, or other secondary materials.

caveats: BLS recycling occupations cover adjacent material-recovery and waste operations as well as merchant wholesalers.
Capital automation of physical sorting is relevant context but is not counted as AI labor substitution unless it avoids current labor within the five-year implementation path.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task mapping across 20 scrap merchants, including time-and-motion studies for scale houses, dispatch, traders, sorters, equipment operators, accounting, and compliance personnel, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: AI exposure is meaningful in quote preparation, commodity and freight monitoring, inbound classification, scale-ticket capture, contract and compliance paperwork, routing, customer service, and back-office reconciliation. Computer vision can also assist sorting and contamination detection. However, much wage cost remains in collection, yard movement, dismantling, equipment operation, maintenance, inspection, and safety-critical physical work; BLS describes manual sorters, equipment, drivers, technicians, managers, and sales roles across recycling operations.

caveats: Ferrous scrap evidence cannot represent all paper, plastic, e-scrap, automotive, and nonferrous subsegments.
A policy goal is not a realized forecast, and EPA's national MSW series excludes substantial industrial scrap.
d5.change_evidence: Material-specific constant-price tonnage forecasts from USGS, EPA, mills, packaging producers, automakers, and state extended-producer-responsibility programs, plus five-year merchant throughput histories, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Secondary materials have durable industrial utility and policy support. USGS estimated 63 million tons of U.S. apparent iron and steel scrap consumption in 2024 versus 62 million in 2023 and describes scrap as vital to steel production. EPA's national goal targets a 50% recycling rate by 2030, creating upside to recoverable volumes. Downside remains from commodity cycles, export restrictions, low virgin-material prices, contamination, product redesign, and the fact that EPA's latest broad MSW figures are old and showed a 32.1% 2018 recycling and composting rate.

caveats: The NAICS definition spans automotive, industrial, and other recyclable materials with materially different economics and liabilities.
The frozen n of 1,987 is an ESTIMATE based on size-class receipts and a general distributor margin bridge, not verified LMM EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A verified in-band company census combining facility permits, material mix, revenue model, customer and supplier concentration, title-taking status, environmental history, and management depth would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: A substantial share of in-band firms should have recurring generator accounts, permits, yards, processing assets, logistics routines, and mill relationships that form transferable operations. Eligibility is reduced by pure brokers, one-site owner-dependent yards, environmental liabilities, opaque inventory practices, cash-control weaknesses, used-parts-led dismantlers, and commodity traders without durable customer service relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423930 ($6,977,402,089) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423930 ($108,858,446,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,058,893 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423930); class <5: 2,831 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,750,414 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $199,022 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,241 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,468,311 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $508,047 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,022 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,018,116 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,139,060 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 965 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,193,863 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,319,342 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 147 firms, avg revenue/firm $122,174,837 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,891,279 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 80 firms, avg revenue/firm $524,844,462 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,674,815 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,987

caveats: Operator requirement does not guarantee that the operator is an independent LMM merchant; mills, waste companies, and generators may vertically integrate.
Pure matching and brokerage functions are more vulnerable to software than yard-based processing.
o.change_evidence: Material-flow studies that separate direct generator-to-mill shipments, digital-broker transactions, toll processing, and merchant-owned inventory, together with customer procurement interviews, would quantify operator displacement.
o.rationale: Nearly all remaining volume requires an accountable operator to collect or receive physical scrap, verify ownership and grade, manage safety and environmental obligations, process and segregate material, finance inventory, arrange transport, and resolve quality disputes. Digital exchanges can replace some broker intermediation, and large generators can contract directly with mills, but software alone cannot perform these physical and liability-bearing functions.

caveats: Commodity-price and grade-mix movements can overwhelm and obscure retained operating benefit.
Local competition, export access, and material purity produce wide variation in bargaining power.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and commodity-level spread waterfalls, bid histories, generator contract clauses, mill settlement data, and win/loss analysis before and after automation would reveal actual sharing and repricing.
q.rationale: Labor and administrative savings are initially retained because counterparties typically price material, grade, freight, and service rather than audit the merchant's cost base. Retention erodes quickly in transparent commodity markets through tighter buy-sell spreads, higher generator rebates, lower service fees, and mill negotiations. Better recovery yield, reliable pickup, compliance, and scarce local processing capacity support more retention than undifferentiated brokerage.

caveats: DOE projects establish technical activity, not broad commercial adoption in LMM scrap merchants.
Implementation feasibility differs sharply between high-throughput processors and small mixed-material yards.
rho.change_evidence: Verified multi-yard deployment results showing labor hours, throughput, recovery yield, contamination, downtime, false-sort rates, and payback for AI vision, robotic sorting, dispatch, and scale-ticket automation would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Routine document, quote, dispatch, and reconciliation copilots can be deployed within five years, while vision-enabled sortation is technically credible. Realization is limited by noisy and variable feedstock, fragmented legacy scale and yard systems, harsh operating environments, safety requirements, integration cost, and the small scale of many yards. DOE-supported projects explicitly remain at development or demonstration stages, arguing against assuming full implementation.

caveats: Shutdowns and establishment exits are not qualifying transfers.
Environmental liabilities may convert an intended sale into an asset liquidation or prevent a transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and specialist broker transactions coded by material, EBITDA, control status, and seller reason, combined with ABS owner-age microdata, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Private scrap yards and regional recyclers face succession, compliance-capital needs, and consolidation incentives that can generate control sales over five years. The estimate excludes closures, asset liquidations, family succession, and minority investments. Public Census owner-age and business-dynamics sources do not isolate qualifying transfers, so this remains bounded judgment.

principal_driver: Physical processing and repeat material flows preserve operator relevance while AI and digitization can reduce commercial, grading, dispatch, and reconciliation work.
principal_weakness: Commodity transparency and operational liabilities can dissipate labor savings and make apparently similar yards differ radically in quality.
